sentences.chinese: 爰盎諫曰：「上素驕淮南王，不為置嚴相傅，以故至此。
sentences.literal: Yuan Ang remonstrated, saying, "The Supreme One has always indulged the King of Huainan, not for him appointed a strict chancellor and tutor; by this cause arrived here."
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang urged him: "You have always spoiled the king of Huainan and never gave him a stern chancellor or tutor. That is how things have come to this pass."

sentences.chinese: 且淮南王為人剛，今暴摧折之，臣恐其逢霧露病死，陛下有殺弟之名，奈何！」
sentences.literal: Moreover the King of Huainan by nature is rigid; now violently crushing and breaking him, your servant fears he may encounter mist and dew and die of illness; Your Majesty will have the name of killing a younger brother—what to do!"
sentences.idiomatic: Moreover he is a proud, unbending man. If you break him by force, I fear he will sicken and die on the road, and you will bear the stain of having murdered your own brother. What then?"

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「吾特苦之耳，令復之。」
sentences.literal: The Supreme One said, "I only meant to distress him; order to restore him."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "I only meant to humble him a little. Send orders to ease his treatment."

sentences.chinese: 淮南王謂侍者曰：「誰謂乃公勇者？
sentences.literal: The King of Huainan told his attendants, saying, "Who says your lord father is brave?
sentences.idiomatic: In the cart Liu Chang said to his attendants, "Who ever called your old master a brave man?

sentences.chinese: 吾以驕不聞過，故至此。」
sentences.literal: I because arrogant did not hear of faults; therefore arrived here."
sentences.idiomatic: I grew arrogant and would hear no criticism of myself—that is how I have come to this."

sentences.chinese: 乃不食而死。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he did not eat and died.
sentences.idiomatic: He refused all food and starved himself to death.

sentences.chinese: 縣傳者不敢發車封。
sentences.literal: Those who relayed by county did not dare open the cart seals.
sentences.idiomatic: At each relay station along the route the escorts dared not break the seals on his wagon.

sentences.chinese: 至雍，雍令發之，以死聞。
sentences.literal: When they reached Yong, the magistrate of Yong opened it and reported by death.
sentences.idiomatic: When the procession reached Yong, the local magistrate opened the wagon and reported that the prisoner was dead.

sentences.chinese: 上悲哭，謂爰盎曰：「吾不從公言，卒亡淮南王。」
sentences.literal: The Supreme One wept in grief and told Yuan Ang, "I did not follow your words; in the end lost the King of Huainan."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor wept and said to Yuan Ang, "I did not heed your warning, and now my brother of Huainan is gone."

sentences.chinese: 盎曰：「淮南王不可奈何，願陛下自寬。」
sentences.literal: Ang said, "The King of Huainan cannot be helped; I hope Your Majesty will himself be broad-minded."
sentences.idiomatic: Ang replied, "Nothing can bring him back now, Your Majesty. You must forgive yourself."

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「為之奈何？」
sentences.literal: The Supreme One said, "What to do about it?"
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "What am I to do?"

sentences.chinese: 曰：「獨斬丞相、御史以謝天下乃可。」
sentences.literal: He said, "Only beheading the chancellor and the imperial counselor to apologize to all under Heaven then can do."
sentences.idiomatic: Ang answered, "Only if you execute the chancellor and the imperial counselor and offer their heads to the empire can you appease the people."

sentences.chinese: 上即令丞相、御史逮諸縣傳淮南王不發封饋侍者，皆棄市，乃以列侯葬淮南王於雍，置守塚三十家。
sentences.literal: The Supreme One immediately ordered the chancellor and imperial counselor to seize all those in the counties who relayed the King of Huainan without opening the seals and who fed the attendants—all executed in the market; thereupon with full marquis rites buried the King of Huainan at Yong, establishing thirty households to guard the tomb.
sentences.idiomatic: He ordered the arrest of every county officer who had relayed the king without breaking the wagon seals and of everyone who had slipped food to his attendants; all were executed in the marketplace. Liu Chang was then buried at Yong with the honors due a full marquis, and thirty families were assigned to tend his grave.

sentences.chinese: 孝文八年，憐淮南王，王有子四人，年皆七八歲，乃封子安為阜陵侯，子勃為安陽侯，子賜為陽周侯，子良為東城侯。
sentences.literal: In the eighth year of Emperor Xiaowen, pitying the King of Huainan, the king having four sons, all aged seven or eight sui, thereupon enfeoffed the son An as marquis of Fuling, the son Bo as marquis of Anyang, the son Ci as marquis of Yangzhou, the son Liang as marquis of Dongcheng.
sentences.idiomatic: In 172 BCE, moved by pity for the late king of Huainan, the emperor enfeoffed his four young sons—each seven or eight years old—as marquises: Liu An at Fuling, Liu Bo at Anyang, Liu Ci at Yangzhou, and Liu Liang at Dongcheng.

sentences.chinese: 十二年，民有作歌歌淮南王曰：「一尺布，尚可縫；
sentences.literal: In the twelfth year, the people had a song made singing the King of Huainan, saying, "One foot of cloth, still can be sewn;
sentences.idiomatic: In 168 BCE a ballad circulated about the king of Huainan: "A foot of cloth can still be stitched together;

sentences.chinese: 一斗粟，尚可春。
sentences.literal: one dou of grain, still can be pounded."
sentences.idiomatic: a peck of grain can still be ground in the mortar;"

sentences.chinese: 兄弟二人，不相容！」
sentences.literal: two brothers in one house, not tolerating each other!"
sentences.idiomatic: yet two brothers under one roof cannot live in peace!"

sentences.chinese: 上聞之曰，昔堯、舜放逐骨肉，周公殺管、蔡，天下稱聖，不以私害公。
sentences.literal: When the Supreme One heard it he said, in former times Yao and Shun banished bone and flesh, the Duke of Zhou killed Guan and Cai; all under Heaven called them sage, not by private harming public.
sentences.idiomatic: When the emperor heard the song he observed that Yao and Shun had banished their kin, and the Duke of Zhou had executed Guan and Cai, yet the world hailed them as sages because they put the common good above private feeling.

sentences.chinese: 天下豈以為我貪淮南地邪！」
sentences.literal: Would all under Heaven think I covet the land of Huainan!"
sentences.idiomatic: Surely no one imagines I murdered my brother for the sake of Huainan!"

sentences.chinese: 乃徙城陽王王淮南故地，而追尊謚淮南王為厲王，置園如諸侯儀。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he moved the King of Chengyang to be king in the former territory of Huainan, and posthumously honored with the posthumous title Prince Li the King of Huainan, establishing a park like the rites of feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: He therefore transferred the king of Chengyang to the old Huainan domain, posthumously titled his late brother the Prince Li of Huainan, and laid out a memorial park for him with the ceremonies due a feudal lord.

sentences.chinese: 十六年，上憐淮南王廢法不軌，自使失國早夭，乃徙淮南王喜復王故城陽，而立厲王三子王淮南故地，三分之：阜陵侯安為淮南王，安陽侯勃為衡山王，陽周侯賜為廬江王，東城侯良前薨，無後。
sentences.literal: In the sixteenth year, the Supreme One pitied the King of Huainan for casting aside law and not being in the track, himself causing loss of state and early death; thereupon moved the King of Huainan Xi again to be king of former Chengyang, and established the three sons of Prince Li as kings in the former territory of Huainan, dividing it in three: the Marquis of Fuling An as King of Huainan, the Marquis of Anyang Bo as King of Hengshan, the Marquis of Yangzhou Ci as King of Lujiang; the Marquis of Dongcheng Liang had earlier died, without posterity.
sentences.idiomatic: In 164 BCE the emperor grieved that his brother had lost his life through lawlessness and an early fall from power. He moved Liu Xi, the current king of Huainan, back to Chengyang and divided the old Huainan lands among Prince Li's three surviving sons: Liu An became king of Huainan, Liu Bo king of Hengshan, and Liu Ci king of Lujiang. The fourth son, Marquis Liu Liang of Dongcheng, had already died without issue.

sentences.chinese: 淮南王安為人好書，鼓琴，不喜戈獵狗馬馳騁，亦欲以行陰德拊循百姓，流名譽。
sentences.literal: The King of Huainan An by nature loved books, strummed the zither, did not like spears hunting dogs horses galloping, also wished by practicing hidden virtue to soothe the hundred surnames, spreading name and reputation.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu An, king of Huainan, loved learning and the zither, cared little for hunting or racing horses and hounds, and hoped to win the people's hearts by quiet acts of kindness and so build a name for benevolence.

sentences.chinese: 招致賓客方術之士數千人，作為《內書》二十一篇，《外書》甚眾，又有《中篇》八卷，言神仙黃白之術，亦二十餘萬言。
sentences.literal: He summoned guests and masters of formulas and techniques numbering several thousand, making the Inner Documents in twenty-one chapters, the Outer Documents very numerous, also having the Middle Pieces in eight scrolls, speaking of transcendents, elixirs, and yellow-white techniques—also more than two hundred thousand graphs.
sentences.idiomatic: He gathered several thousand retainers and adepts of esoteric arts, produced twenty-one chapters of the Inner Writings and a vast body of Outer Writings, plus eight scrolls of the Middle Treatise on immortals and the alchemy of gold—all told, well over two hundred thousand characters.

sentences.chinese: 時武帝方好藝文，以安屬為諸父，辯博善為文辭，甚尊重之。
sentences.literal: At that time Emperor Wu was just then fond of arts and letters; because An belonged to the class of fathers' generation, was eloquent and broad and good at literary phrasing, he greatly honored and respected him.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wu was cultivating literature and the arts; Liu An, as an uncle on his father's side, was learned, eloquent, and a fine writer, and the emperor treated him with marked respect.

sentences.chinese: 每為報書及賜，常召司馬相如等視草乃遣。
sentences.literal: Whenever replying to letters and bestowals, he constantly summoned Sima Xiangru and others to review the draft before dispatching.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever he drafted replies to the throne or imperial gifts, the emperor would often call in Sima Xiangru and others to polish the text before it was sent.

sentences.chinese: 初，安入朝，獻所作《內篇》，新出，上愛秘之。
sentences.literal: At first, when An entered court audience, he presented what he had made, the Inner Chapters, newly issued; the Supreme One loved and kept them secret.
sentences.idiomatic: On Liu An's first visit to court he presented his newly finished Inner Chapters; the emperor prized them and had them locked away in the palace library.

sentences.chinese: 使為《離騷傳》，旦受詔，日食時上。
sentences.literal: He ordered him to make a Commentary on Li sao; at dawn receiving the edict, by the time of the sun meal he presented it.
sentences.idiomatic: He commissioned a commentary on the Li sao; Liu An received the order at dawn and presented the finished work by breakfast.

sentences.chinese: 又獻《頌德》及《長安都國頌》。
sentences.literal: He also presented In Praise of Virtue and In Praise of the Metropolis and States of Chang'an.
sentences.idiomatic: He also offered his Hymn in Praise of Virtue and his Ode to Chang'an and the Commanderies and Kingdoms.

sentences.chinese: 每宴見，談說得失及方技賦頌，昏莫然後罷。
sentences.literal: Whenever feasting audience, discussing right and wrong and formulas, techniques, rhapsodies, and hymns, at dusk only then ceased.
sentences.idiomatic: At each informal audience they would debate policy, literature, and occult lore until dusk before breaking off.

sentences.chinese: 安初入朝，雅善太尉武安侯，武安侯迎之霸上，與語曰：「方今上無太子，王親高皇帝孫，行仁義，天下莫不聞。
sentences.literal: When An first entered court audience, he was on good terms with the Grand Commandant Marquis of Wu'an; the Marquis of Wu'an met him at Bashang and spoke with him, saying, "At present the Supreme One has no heir apparent; the king is in person a grandson of the Supreme Emperor, practices benevolence and right—none under Heaven has not heard."
sentences.idiomatic: On Liu An's first visit to the capital he struck up a friendship with Tian Fen, the Marquis of Wu'an, who met him at Bashang and said, "The emperor has no heir. You are a grandson of the Gaozu Emperor in the direct line, and your reputation for benevolence is known everywhere."

sentences.chinese: 宮車一日晏駕，非王尚誰立者！」
sentences.literal: When the palace carriage one day goes on the late drive, if not the king, who else is to be established!"
sentences.idiomatic: When the imperial carriage one day makes its final journey, who else should succeed but you?"

sentences.chinese: 淮南王大喜，厚遺武安侯寶賂。
sentences.literal: The King of Huainan was greatly pleased and richly presented the Marquis of Wu'an with treasures and bribes.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu An was overjoyed and showered the Marquis of Wu'an with rich gifts.

sentences.chinese: 其群臣賓客，江淮間多輕薄，以厲王遷死感激安。
sentences.literal: His host of ministers and guests—between the Yangzi and Huai many were frivolous—because Prince Li had died in banishment, were stirred in sympathy toward An.
sentences.idiomatic: Many of his retainers between the Yangzi and the Huai were reckless men who still burned with indignation over Prince Li's death on the road to exile and fed Liu An's ambitions.

sentences.chinese: 建元六年，彗星見，淮南王心怪之。
sentences.literal: In the sixth year of Jianyuan, a comet appeared; the King of Huainan in his heart thought it strange.
sentences.idiomatic: In 135 BCE a comet appeared, and the king of Huainan took it for an omen.

sentences.chinese: 或說王曰：「先吳軍時，彗星出，長數尺，然尚流血千里。
sentences.literal: Someone persuaded the king, saying, "Before the Wu campaign, a comet appeared, several feet long, yet still blood flowed a thousand li.
sentences.idiomatic: A retainer told him, "Before the revolt of Wu, the comet was only a few feet long, yet still the fighting bloodied the empire for a thousand li.

sentences.chinese: 今彗星竟天，天下兵當大起。」
sentences.literal: Now the comet fills the sky; arms under Heaven ought greatly to rise."
sentences.idiomatic: This one stretches across the whole sky—great war must be coming."

sentences.chinese: 王心以為上無太子，天下有變，諸侯並爭，愈益治攻戰具，積金錢賂遺郡國。
sentences.literal: The king in his heart thought that because the Supreme One had no heir apparent, if all under Heaven had change, the feudal lords together would contend; he increasingly prepared tools for attack and war, piled gold and cash to bribe and gift commanderies and kingdoms.
sentences.idiomatic: Convinced that the lack of an heir would throw the realm into turmoil among the princes, he stepped up the manufacture of weapons, hoarded gold, and sent bribes to officials in commanderies and kingdoms across the empire.

sentences.chinese: 游士妄作妖言阿諛王，王喜，多賜予之。
sentences.literal: Wandering scholars recklessly made prodigious words flattering the king; the king was pleased and much bestowed on them.
sentences.idiomatic: Itinerant scholars fed him wild prophecies and flattery; pleased, he rewarded them handsomely.

sentences.chinese: 王有女陵，彗有口。
sentences.literal: The king had a daughter Ling; comet had a mouth.
sentences.idiomatic: The king had a daughter named Ling, sharp-tongued as a comet's tail.

sentences.chinese: 王愛陵，多予金錢，為中□長安，約結上左右。
sentences.literal: The king loved Ling, much gave her gold and cash, for her to dwell in Chang'an, covenanting and knotting those to the left and right of the Supreme One.
sentences.idiomatic: He doted on her, supplied her generously with cash, and stationed her in Chang'an to buy friends at court.

sentences.chinese: 元朔二年，上賜淮南王几杖，不朝。
sentences.literal: In the second year of Yuanshuo, the Supreme One bestowed on the King of Huainan armrest and staff, not requiring court audience.
sentences.idiomatic: In 127 BCE the emperor granted him a stool and staff of age, exempting him from the duty of attending court.

sentences.chinese: 後荼愛幸，生子遷為太子，取皇太后外孫修成君女為太子妃。
sentences.literal: Queen Tu received favor, bore a son Qian who became heir apparent; he took the daughter of the Empress Dowager's maternal grandson Lady Xiucheng as heir apparent's consort.
sentences.idiomatic: His favored consort Tu bore a son, Liu Qian, whom he made crown prince. The prince took as his consort the daughter of Lady Xiucheng, a granddaughter of the empress dowager on the maternal side.

sentences.chinese: 王謀為反具，畏太子妃知而內洩事，乃與太子謀，令詐不愛，三月不同席。
sentences.literal: The king plotted to make tools for rebellion, feared the heir apparent's consort would know and inside leak the affair; thereupon plotted with the heir apparent, ordering him falsely not to love, three months not sharing the mat.
sentences.idiomatic: Because he was preparing rebellion and feared the crown princess might learn of it and report to the palace, he plotted with the crown prince to feign estrangement: for three months the prince would not share his wife's bed.

sentences.chinese: 王陽怒太子，閉使與妃同內，終不近妃。
sentences.literal: The king feigned anger at the heir apparent, shut him making him with the consort in the same inner quarters, yet to the end did not approach the consort.
sentences.idiomatic: The king pretended to punish the prince by confining him with his wife, yet the prince never touched her.

sentences.chinese: 妃求去，王乃上書謝歸之。
sentences.literal: The consort requested to depart; the king thereupon submitted a memorial apologizing and sending her back.
sentences.idiomatic: When she begged leave to return home, the king memorialized the throne with an apology and sent her back to the capital.

sentences.chinese: 後荼、太子遷及女陵擅國權，奪民田宅，妄致系人。
sentences.literal: Queen Tu, the heir apparent Qian, and the daughter Ling usurped state power, seized the people's fields and dwellings, recklessly caused men to be bound.
sentences.idiomatic: Queen Tu, Crown Prince Qian, and Princess Ling abused their power at court, seized commoners' land and houses, and threw innocent men into prison on whim.

sentences.chinese: 太子學用劍，自以為人莫及，聞郎中雷被巧，召與戲，被壹再辭讓，誤中太子。
sentences.literal: The heir apparent studied using the sword, considered himself unmatched by others; hearing that the gentleman Lei Bei was skillful, summoned him to fence; Bei repeatedly declined and yielded, by mistake struck the heir apparent.
sentences.idiomatic: The crown prince had studied fencing and believed no one his equal. Hearing that the gentleman Lei Bei was a master of the blade, he summoned him to bout. Lei Bei declined again and again, but in the exchange he accidentally cut the prince.

sentences.chinese: 太子怒，被恐。
sentences.literal: The heir apparent was angry; Bei feared.
sentences.idiomatic: The prince flew into a rage, and Lei Bei was terrified.

sentences.chinese: 此時有欲從軍者輒詣長安，被即願奮擊匈奴。
sentences.literal: At this time whoever wished to follow the army always went to Chang'an; Bei then wished to strive to strike the Xiongnu.
sentences.idiomatic: A general call had gone out for volunteers to report to Chang'an for service against the Xiongnu, and Lei Bei volunteered at once.

sentences.chinese: 太子數惡被，王使郎中令斥免，欲以禁後。
sentences.literal: The heir apparent repeatedly slandered Bei; the king ordered the supervisor of gentlemen to reprimand and dismiss him, wishing thereby to forbid what followed.
sentences.idiomatic: The prince slandered Lei Bei repeatedly until the king had the supervisor of gentlemen dismiss him from office, hoping to deter him from leaving.

sentences.chinese: 元朔五年，被遂亡之長安，上書自明。
sentences.literal: In the fifth year of Yuanshuo, Bei thereupon fled to Chang'an and submitted a memorial to clear himself.
sentences.idiomatic: In 124 BCE Lei Bei fled to the capital and memorialized the throne in his own defense.

sentences.chinese: 事下廷尉、河南。
sentences.literal: The affair was sent down to the commandant of justice and Henan.
sentences.idiomatic: The case was referred to the commandant of justice and the Henan governor.

sentences.chinese: 河南治，逮淮南太子，王、王后計欲毋遣太子，遂發兵。
sentences.literal: Henan prosecuted, summoned the heir apparent of Huainan; the king and queen plotted wishing not to send the heir apparent, thereupon raised troops.
sentences.idiomatic: When Henan ordered the crown prince brought in for questioning, the king and queen plotted to refuse and considered raising troops at once.

sentences.chinese: 計未定，猶與十餘日。
sentences.literal: The plan was not settled; they hesitated more than ten days.
sentences.idiomatic: They wavered for more than ten days without reaching a decision.

sentences.chinese: 會有詔即訊太子。
sentences.literal: It happened that there was an edict to immediately question the heir apparent.
sentences.idiomatic: Then an edict arrived ordering the crown prince to be examined on the spot.

sentences.chinese: 淮南相怒壽春丞留太子逮不遣，劾不敬。
sentences.literal: The chancellor of Huainan was angry that the assistant of Shouchun detained the heir apparent's summons and did not send him, impeached for irreverence.
sentences.idiomatic: The chancellor of Huainan, furious that the assistant magistrate of Shouchun was holding the prince despite the writ of arrest, impeached him for disrespect.

sentences.chinese: 王請相，相不聽。
sentences.literal: The king requested the chancellor; the chancellor did not heed.
sentences.idiomatic: The king appealed to the chancellor, who refused to yield.

sentences.chinese: 王使人上書告相，事下廷尉治。
sentences.literal: The king sent men to submit a memorial accusing the chancellor; the affair was sent down to the commandant of justice for prosecution.
sentences.idiomatic: The king retaliated with a memorial accusing the chancellor, and the matter went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.chinese: 從跡連王，王使人候司。
sentences.literal: Following the traces connected the king; the king sent men to watch and spy.
sentences.idiomatic: The investigation soon pointed toward the king himself, who posted spies to watch for the next move from the capital.

sentences.chinese: 漢公卿請逮捕治王，王恐，欲發兵。
sentences.literal: Han dukes and ministers requested to arrest and prosecute the king; the king feared, wished to raise troops.
sentences.idiomatic: When the high ministers of Han demanded his arrest, panic seized him and he again thought of rising in arms.

sentences.chinese: 太子遷謀曰：「漢使即逮王，令人衣衛士衣，持戟居王旁，有非是者，即刺殺之，臣亦使人刺殺淮南中尉，乃舉兵，未晚也。」
sentences.literal: The heir apparent Qian plotted, saying, "When Han envoys come to seize the king, order men to wear guards' robes, hold halberds and stand by the king's side; whoever is not right, immediately stab and kill him; your servant also will send men to stab and kill the commandant of Huainan, then raise troops—not late."
sentences.idiomatic: Crown Prince Qian proposed, "If Han envoys come to arrest you, Father, dress our men as palace guards and station them at your side with halberds. At the first wrong move, kill them. I will have our commandant of Huainan assassinated at the same moment, then we can rise—it will not be too late."

sentences.chinese: 是時上不許公卿，而遣漢中尉宏即訊驗王。
sentences.literal: At this time the Supreme One did not permit the dukes and ministers, but dispatched the Han commandant of the capital Hong to immediately question and examine the king.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor rejected the ministers' demand for arrest and instead sent the commandant of the capital, Hong, to question the king on the spot.

sentences.chinese: 王視漢中尉顏色和，問斥雷被事耳，自度無何，不發。
sentences.literal: The king observed the Han commandant's expression was mild, asked only about the matter of reprimanding Lei Bei; he measured himself as without issue, did not rise.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu An saw that the commandant's manner was mild and that he asked only about the Lei Bei affair; reassured that nothing worse was afoot, he dropped the plan to revolt.

sentences.chinese: 中尉還，以聞。
sentences.literal: The commandant returned and reported.
sentences.idiomatic: Hong returned to the capital and made his report.

sentences.chinese: 公卿治者曰：「淮南王安雍閼求奮擊匈奴者雷被等，格明詔，當棄市。」
sentences.literal: The prosecuting dukes and ministers said, "The King of Huainan An blocked and barred those who sought to strive to strike the Xiongnu such as Lei Bei, resisting the clear edict—ought to be executed in the market."
sentences.idiomatic: The ministers ruled that Liu An had obstructed volunteers such as Lei Bei who wished to fight the Xiongnu, defying an explicit imperial order—a capital offense.

sentences.chinese: 詔不許。
sentences.literal: An edict did not permit.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor refused approval.

sentences.chinese: 請廢勿王，上不許。
sentences.literal: They requested to depose and not let him be king; the Supreme One did not permit.
sentences.idiomatic: They asked that he be deposed entirely; again the emperor refused.

sentences.chinese: 請削五縣，可二縣。
sentences.literal: They requested to cut five counties; he approved two counties.
sentences.idiomatic: They proposed stripping him of five counties; he allowed two to be taken.

sentences.chinese: 使中尉宏赦其罪，罰以削地。
sentences.literal: He ordered Commandant Hong to pardon his crime, punishing by cutting territory.
sentences.idiomatic: He sent Commandant Hong to announce a pardon and commute the sentence to loss of territory.

sentences.chinese: 中尉入淮南界，宣言赦王。
sentences.literal: The commandant entered Huainan territory and publicly proclaimed pardon of the king.
sentences.idiomatic: Hong crossed into Huainan and publicly proclaimed the king's pardon.

sentences.chinese: 王初聞公卿請誅之，未知得削地，聞漢使來，恐其捕之，乃與太子謀如前計。
sentences.literal: The king at first heard the dukes and ministers requested to execute him, did not yet know he would obtain cutting of territory; hearing Han envoys were coming, feared they would seize him; thereupon plotted with the heir apparent as the former plan.
sentences.idiomatic: When Liu An first heard the ministers demand his death, before he learned that the sentence would be reduced to loss of fiefs, the approach of Han envoys convinced him he was to be arrested, and he revived the plot with his son.

sentences.chinese: 中尉至，即賀王，王以故不發。
sentences.literal: When the commandant arrived, immediately congratulated the king; the king for this reason did not rise.
sentences.idiomatic: When Hong arrived he congratulated the king on his reprieve, and Liu An therefore did not rebel.

sentences.chinese: 其後自傷曰：「吉行仁義見削地，寡人甚恥之。」
sentences.literal: Afterward he wounded himself, saying, "I who practice benevolence and right see my territory cut—I am deeply shamed."
sentences.idiomatic: Afterward he brooded, "I have walked the path of benevolence, yet I am punished with loss of land—this is a humiliation I cannot bear."

sentences.chinese: 為反謀益甚。
sentences.literal: His plotting for rebellion became more extreme.
sentences.idiomatic: His rebellious designs grew more desperate than ever.

sentences.chinese: 諸使者道長安來，為妄言，言上無男，即喜：言漢廷治，有男，即怒，以為妄言，非也。
sentences.literal: The various envoys coming by way from Chang'an, he had them speak false words—if they said the Supreme One had no son, then he was pleased; if they said the Han court was orderly and had a son, then he was angry, considering it false words, not right.
sentences.idiomatic: He coached envoys from the capital to bring him rumors: if they said the emperor had no son, he rejoiced; if they reported an orderly court and the birth of an heir, he flew into a rage and dismissed their words as lies.

sentences.chinese: 日夜與左吳等按輿地圖，部署兵所從入。
sentences.literal: Day and night with Zuo Wu and others he consulted carriage-and-earth maps, deploying where troops should enter from.
sentences.idiomatic: Day and night he pored over maps with Zuo Wu and others, marking the routes by which his army would march.

sentences.chinese: 王曰：「上無太子，宮車即晏駕，大臣必征膠東王，不即常山王，諸侯並爭，吾可以無備乎！
sentences.literal: The king said, "The Supreme One has no heir apparent; when the palace carriage then goes on the late drive, the great ministers are sure to summon the King of Jiaodong, if not the King of Changshan; the feudal lords together will contend—how can I be without preparation!
sentences.idiomatic: He declared, "The emperor has no heir. When the imperial carriage makes its final journey, the ministers will surely summon the king of Jiaodong or failing him the king of Changshan. The princes will tear one another apart—can I afford to be unprepared?

sentences.chinese: 且吾高帝孫，親行仁義，陛下遇我厚，吾能忍之；
sentences.literal: Moreover I am the Supreme Emperor's grandson, in person practice benevolence and right; Your Majesty's treatment of me is thick—can I endure it;
sentences.idiomatic: I am a grandson of the Gaozu Emperor and have walked the path of benevolence. The Son of Heaven has treated me generously—so far I have borne it;

sentences.chinese: 萬世之後，吾寧能北面事豎子乎！」
sentences.literal: after ten thousand generations, how could I face north and serve a stripling!"
sentences.idiomatic: but when he is gone, am I to bow northward and serve some whelp of his line?"

sentences.chinese: 王有孽子不害，最長，王不愛，後、太子皆不以為子兄數。
sentences.literal: The king had a bastard son Buhei, eldest in age; the king did not love him; the queen and heir apparent both did not treat him in the reckoning of son and elder brother.
sentences.idiomatic: The king had an illegitimate eldest son named Liu Buhei, whom he did not favor; neither the queen nor the crown prince acknowledged him as brother or son in the proper order.

sentences.chinese: 不害子建，材高有氣，常怨望太子不省其父。
sentences.literal: Buhei's son Jian, talented and high-spirited, constantly resented the heir apparent for not visiting his father.
sentences.idiomatic: Buhei's son Jian was proud and able; he bitterly resented the crown prince for slighting his father.

sentences.chinese: 時，諸侯皆得分子弟為侯，淮南王有兩子，一子為太子，而建父不得為侯。
sentences.literal: At the time, all feudal lords could divide sons and younger brothers as marquises; the King of Huainan had two sons, one son was heir apparent, yet Jian's father could not become marquis.
sentences.idiomatic: Princes were then allowed to enfeoff younger sons as marquises, but though the king of Huainan had two grown sons—one the crown prince—Jian's father had received no such title.

sentences.chinese: 陰結交，欲害太子，以其父代之。
sentences.literal: Secretly he formed alliances, wishing to harm the heir apparent, thereby his father replacing him.
sentences.idiomatic: Jian formed a secret faction, hoping to destroy the crown prince and put his own father in his place.

sentences.chinese: 太子知之，數捕系笞建。
sentences.literal: The heir apparent knew it, repeatedly seized, bound, and flogged Jian.
sentences.idiomatic: When the crown prince learned of it, he had Jian seized time and again and beaten with the bamboo.

sentences.chinese: 建具知太子之欲謀殺漢中尉，即使所善壽春嚴正上書天子曰：「毒藥苦口利病，忠言逆耳利行。
sentences.literal: Jian fully knew the heir apparent's wish to plot killing the Han commandant; immediately had one he was close to, Yan Zheng of Shouchun, submit a memorial to the Son of Heaven, saying, "Poison medicine is bitter in the mouth but benefits illness; loyal words offend the ear but benefit conduct."
sentences.idiomatic: Jian knew the crown prince had plotted to murder the Han commandant. He had his friend Yan Zheng of Shouchun memorialize the throne: "Bitter medicine heals sickness; unwelcome counsel saves the state."

sentences.chinese: 今淮南王孫建材能高，淮南王后荼、荼子遷常疾害建。
sentences.literal: Now the King of Huainan's grandson Jian's talent and ability are high; the King of Huainan's queen Tu and Tu's son Qian constantly hate and harm Jian.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Huainan's grandson Jian is a man of outstanding ability, yet Queen Tu and her son Qian persecute him relentlessly.

sentences.chinese: 建父不害無罪，擅數系，欲殺之。
sentences.literal: Jian's father Buhei was without guilt; yet he was repeatedly seized on authority, they wished to kill him.
sentences.idiomatic: Jian's father Buhei is innocent, yet they have jailed him again and again and mean to put him to death.

sentences.chinese: 今建在，可征問，具知淮南王陰事。」
sentences.literal: Now Jian is present; he can be summoned and questioned—he fully knows the King of Huainan's secret affairs."
sentences.idiomatic: Jian is still at hand and can be examined under oath—he knows every secret of the king of Huainan's plotting.

sentences.chinese: 書既聞，上以其事下廷尉、河南治。
sentences.literal: When the memorial had been heard, the Supreme One sent the affair down to the commandant of justice and Henan for prosecution.
sentences.idiomatic: When the memorial reached him, the emperor referred the matter to the commandant of justice and the Henan authorities.

sentences.chinese: 是歲元朔六年也。
sentences.literal: This year was the sixth year of Yuanshuo.
sentences.idiomatic: This was the sixth year of Yuanshuo (123 BCE).

sentences.chinese: 故辟陽侯孫審卿善丞相公孫弘，怨淮南厲王殺其大父，陰求淮南事而□之於弘。
sentences.literal: The former Marquis of Piyang's grandson Shen Qing was on good terms with Chancellor Gongsun Hong, resented the Prince Li of Huainan for killing his great-grandfather, secretly sought Huainan affairs and [missing graph] them to Hong.
sentences.idiomatic: Shen Qing, grandson of the Marquis of Piyang, was friendly with Chancellor Gongsun Hong. He nursed a grudge because Prince Li of Huainan had murdered his grandfather, and he quietly gathered evidence of Liu An's plotting to pass to Hong.

sentences.chinese: 弘乃疑淮南有畔逆計，深探其獄。
sentences.literal: Hong thereupon suspected Huainan had a treasonous plot, deeply probed the case.
sentences.idiomatic: Hong began to suspect a rebellion in Huainan and pushed the investigation to the bottom.

sentences.chinese: 河南治建，辭引太子及黨與。
sentences.literal: Henan prosecuted Jian; his testimony implicated the heir apparent and accomplices.
sentences.idiomatic: Under interrogation in Henan, Jian's confession implicated the crown prince and his confederates.

sentences.chinese: 初，王數以舉兵謀問伍被，被常諫之，以吳、楚七國為效。
sentences.literal: At first, the king repeatedly asked Wu Bei about plans to raise troops; Bei constantly remonstrated, taking Wu and Chu seven states as a lesson.
sentences.idiomatic: At first Liu An often consulted Wu Bei about rebellion, but Wu Bei always dissuaded him, citing the fate of the seven kingdoms of Wu and Chu.

sentences.chinese: 王引陳勝、吳廣，被復言形勢不同，必敗亡。
sentences.literal: The king cited Chen Sheng and Wu Guang; Bei again said the situation was different, sure to be defeated and destroyed.
sentences.idiomatic: The king countered with Chen Sheng and Wu Guang; Wu Bei replied that the times were not the same and such a rising would end in ruin.

sentences.chinese: 及建見治，王恐國陰事洩，欲發，復問被，被為言發兵權變。
sentences.literal: When Jian came under prosecution, the king feared the state's secret affairs would leak, wished to rise, again asked Bei; Bei for him spoke of raising troops with stratagems and shifts.
sentences.idiomatic: When Jian fell under investigation, Liu An feared exposure and again asked Wu Bei about revolt; this time Wu Bei outlined stratagems for raising troops.

sentences.chinese: 語在《被傳》。
sentences.literal: The words are in the Biography of Bei.
sentences.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Wu Bei's biography.

sentences.chinese: 於是王銳欲發，乃令官奴入宮中，作皇帝璽，丞相、御史大夫、將軍、吏中二千石、都官令、丞印，及旁近郡太守、都尉印，漢使節法冠。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the king keenly wished to rise; he ordered palace slaves to enter the palace, forge the Son of Heaven's seal, the chancellor's, imperial counselor's, generals', mid-two-thousand-dan officials', capital bureau director and assistant seals, and the seals of neighboring commanderies' governors and commandants, Han envoy credentials and law caps.
sentences.idiomatic: Burning to act, he sent slaves into the palace to forge the imperial seal, seals for chancellor, imperial counselor, generals, mid-ranking officials, capital bureau directors and assistants, seals of neighboring governors and commandants, and Han credentials and court caps.

sentences.chinese: 欲如伍被計，使人為得罪而西，事大將軍、丞相；
sentences.literal: Wishing to follow Wu Bei's plan, he sent men to feign guilt and go west, serving the grand general and chancellor;
sentences.idiomatic: Following Wu Bei's scheme, he planned to send agents westward under pretense of disgrace to attach themselves to the grand general and the chancellor;

sentences.chinese: 一日發兵，即刺大將軍衛青，而說丞相弘下之，如發蒙耳。
sentences.literal: the day they raise troops, immediately stab Grand General Wei Qing, and persuade Chancellor Hong to submit—like lifting a blind from the eyes.
sentences.idiomatic: On the day of the uprising they would assassinate Grand General Wei Qing and win Chancellor Hong to their side—as easy as lifting a blindfold.
